title: On the Origin of Species
excerpt: The foundational work of evolutionary biology, presenting natural selection as the primary mechanism driving the descent of all species from common ancestors.
keyIdeas: Natural selection — the differential survival and reproduction of individuals due to variation in heritable traits
Common descent — all organisms share a common ancestor, branching into new species over vast geological time
Gradualism — evolution proceeds through the slow accumulation of small heritable changes across generations
The tree of life — the branching pattern of relationships among species, inferred from comparative anatomy, embryology, and the fossil record
keyTakeaways: Species are not immutable — they change over time through descent with modification
The struggle for existence, combined with heritable variation, inevitably produces adaptation
All observable organic diversity can be explained by natural laws without recourse to special creation
The geological record, geographical distribution, and comparative morphology all converge on the same evolutionary explanation
whoShouldRead: Anyone who wants to understand the scientific basis of modern biology
Students of evolutionary biology, genetics, paleontology, and ecology
Readers interested in the history of science and paradigm-shifting ideas
Philosophers and theologians grappling with humanity's place in nature
whoShouldSkip: Creationists who reject empirical science outright
Readers seeking a modern genetics-based account of evolution (try The Selfish Gene or Why Evolution Is True instead)
Those unwilling to engage with 19th-century scientific prose
